phrasalVerbs.definition: To say something suddenly and without thinking about the effect it will have, usually because you are nervous or excited.
examples: She blurted out the secret before realising everyone was listening.
In his excitement, he blurted out the surprise party plans.
Try not to blurt out your opinions during the formal meeting.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To be spoken, heard, or understood in a particular way.
examples: I didn't mean to be rude—it just came out wrong.
Her frustration came out clearly in her tone of voice.
The apology came out sounding insincere despite his best efforts.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To say something suddenly, usually something that surprises or shocks people.
examples: She came out with a brilliant solution no one had considered.
He always comes out with the most unexpected observations.
During the interview, the candidate came out with some controversial views.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To make people understand something; to communicate an idea effectively.
examples: The teacher used diagrams to get across the complex concept.
It's difficult to get across how important this deadline is.
His presentation failed to get across the urgency of the situation.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To talk about something that is intended to be a secret; to reveal information.
examples: Don't let on that we know about their engagement.
She never let on how much the criticism had hurt her.
He didn't let on that he had already seen the confidential report.

fillInExercises.sentence: In her nervousness, she ___ the answer before the question was finished.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Blurt out' means to say something suddenly without thinking, often due to nervousness.

fillInExercises.sentence: I tried to explain but my words just didn't ___ out right.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come out' refers to how words are spoken or understood.

fillInExercises.sentence: The speaker used several examples to ___ his main point.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get across' means to communicate an idea so others understand it.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't ___ on that you know about the surprise party.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Let on' means to reveal something that should be kept secret.

fillInExercises.sentence: He suddenly ___ an observation that left everyone speechless.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come out with' means to say something unexpected that surprises people.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ out the news before anyone could stop her.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Blurt out' means to say something suddenly without thinking.

fillInExercises.sentence: Her criticism didn't ___ the way she had intended it to.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come out' describes how something is expressed or perceived.

fillInExercises.sentence: It took several attempts to ___ across the importance of the project.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get across' means to successfully communicate an idea to others.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: I can't believe I [blurt out] the news about the promotion!

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: What happened? How did it [come out]?

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: I was so excited that I just [come out with] it during lunch.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Did you [let on] that you knew before the official announcement?

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Unfortunately, yes. I couldn't [get across] that I hadn't meant to reveal it.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Well, at least try not to [let on] that you feel guilty about it.

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: How did you think my presentation [come out]?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: You definitely [get across] the key points effectively.

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: I nearly [blurt out] the wrong statistics at one point.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: I noticed, but you recovered well. It didn't [let on] that you were nervous.

lines.speaker: Sarah
lines.text: Thanks! I'll try to [get across] the financial data more clearly next time.
